sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian agriculture departments, horticultural research, and recognised stone-fruit and nursery industry sources.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing plums are the same: full sun, free-draining soil, the right variety for your winters, and usually a compatible second tree. Plums come in two families that behave differently, the juicy Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) that suit most gardens and the sweeter, later European plums and prunes (Prunus domestica) for cooler districts. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you live; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Australian plums split into two species that do not interchange. Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) are the common garden plums: juicy, round and best eaten fresh, including Santa Rosa, the red-fleshed blood plums (Satsuma, Mariposa, Ruby Blood), Narrabeen and the big Elephant Heart. European plums (Prunus domestica) are smaller, sweeter and firmer, used fresh, cooked or dried, and include the prunes (D'Agen, Robe de Sergeant, Sugar) and the gages (Greengage). For warm, mild-winter gardens there are low-chill Japanese plums, the Florida-bred Gulf series (Gulf Ruby, Gulf Gold, Gulf Blaze, Gulf Beauty) and A-Okay. Pluots and plumcots such as Flavor Supreme are plum and apricot crosses for something a little different. Decide what you want to eat, match it to your winter chill, then pick a named variety.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Most plums crop far better with a second, compatible variety nearby, and many will not set a worthwhile crop alone. The key rule is that Japanese and European plums do not pollinate each other, because they flower at different times and are not genetically compatible, so always pair Japanese with Japanese and European with European. Santa Rosa is the exception worth knowing: it is partially self-fertile and is the standard pollinator for other Japanese plums, so it crops on its own and helps everything around it. Most other Japanese plums (Satsuma, Mariposa, Narrabeen, the blood plums and the Gulf series) need a partner that flowers at the same time. Many European plums and gages are self-fertile, though some, including the D'Agen prune, set more heavily with a second European variety. If you have room for only one tree, choose Santa Rosa, a self-fertile European, or a multi-graft tree that carries two or three compatible varieties on one trunk.

sections.heading: Chill hours: match the tree to your winter
sections.body: Plums need a spell of winter cold (chilling) to flower and fruit properly, and the amount varies hugely by type. As a rough guide, the low-chill Gulf series and A-Okay need only around 250 to 400 hours below 7 degrees, standard Japanese plums such as Santa Rosa, Satsuma and Mariposa want roughly 400 to 700 hours, and European plums and prunes need the most, around 800 hours or more. Plant a high-chill European prune in a mild coastal garden and it will grow but barely fruit; plant a low-chill Gulf plum in a cold district and it can blossom too early and lose the crop to frost. Check the chill rating on the label against your district before you buy.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Plant plums in full sun in deep, well-drained soil, ideally slightly acidic at around pH 6.0 to 6.5. They crop best with steady moisture but will not tolerate waterlogging, so avoid any spot where water pools and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy clay. Bare-root (dormant) trees go in over winter, the cheapest way to buy them; potted trees can be planted through much of the year as long as you water them through summer (see our guide on when to plant). Keep the graft union above the soil and mulch well, keeping the mulch off the trunk.

sections.heading: Water, feeding and pruning
sections.body: Water young and fruiting trees deeply through spring and summer, and never let them dry out in the last six to eight weeks before harvest, when the fruit fills and sweetens, because dry soil at that stage means small fruit and dropped fruit; in a hot, dry spell that can mean watering two or three times a week. Plums are only moderate feeders, so once a year is enough: a complete fruit-tree fertiliser, or well-rotted manure, in late winter before growth starts, leaning to a higher-potassium product for fruiting rather than a high-nitrogen one, since too much nitrogen pushes leafy growth and water shoots at the expense of fruit. In warm districts it pays to hold the heavier nitrogen feeds until after harvest. They like a slightly acidic soil (about pH 6.0 to 6.5) and a yearly mulch kept off the trunk. Prune plums to an open vase shape, three to five main branches around an open centre, so light and air reach the fruit and brown rot is kept down; Japanese plums fruit on spurs and one-year-old wood and are usually tidied in summer after harvest, while European plums fruit on longer-lived spurs.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Plums ripen through summer, and for the best flavour you want them to colour up and soften on the tree. They are a climacteric fruit, so they keep softening after picking, but the sugar is laid down while they hang on the tree, so a plum picked hard and early goes soft without ever turning truly sweet. Pick when the fruit is fully coloured and gives slightly to a gentle squeeze. Plums do not keep long, about a week in the fridge for eating, so pick over the tree every few days as they ripen. A grafted, named tree usually starts bearing within about two to four years.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than a seedling. Plums do not come true from seed, so seedlings give unpredictable fruit and take years longer to bear, while grafted, named stock fruits sooner and to type. The rootstock matters too: Myrobalan and Marianna suit most soils (Marianna copes better with heavy ground), and a low-chill peach rootstock is used for subtropical districts, so ask for a dwarfing rootstock if space is tight. A multi-graft plum is a tidy way to fit two or three compatible varieties, and their pollination, into one small-garden tree. The in-stock table above shows the named plums available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Do I need two plum trees to get fruit?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Most Japanese plums need a second, compatible Japanese variety flowering at the same time, and Japanese and European plums do not pollinate each other. The main exception is Santa Rosa, which is partially self-fertile and also pollinates other Japanese plums. Many European plums and gages are self-fertile. If you have room for only one tree, choose Santa Rosa, a self-fertile European, or a multi-graft tree.

faqs.q: Can I grow a plum where winters are mild?
faqs.a: Yes, if you choose a low-chill variety. The Florida-bred Gulf series (Gulf Ruby, Gulf Gold, Gulf Blaze, Gulf Beauty) and A-Okay need only around 250 to 400 hours of winter chill and fruit well in warm coastal and subtropical gardens, while high-chill European prunes need around 800 hours or more and suit cool districts.

A blood plum is a red-fleshed Japanese plum (Prunus salicina), red right through to the stone rather than just in the skin. Satsuma, Mariposa and Ruby Blood are common blood plums, prized for rich, sweet, juicy flesh.

faqs.q: How long until a plum tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted, named plum usually starts bearing within about two to four years, sometimes a little longer depending on the variety, rootstock and conditions. Seedlings take much longer and the fruit is unpredictable, which is why named, grafted stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a Japanese and a European plum?
faqs.a: Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) are the larger, juicy, round plums best eaten fresh; they need less winter chill and are the usual garden choice. European plums (Prunus domestica) are smaller, sweeter and firmer, ripen later, need more chill, and include the prunes for drying and the gages for cooking and jam.

WA.intro: Growing plums in Western Australia. The south-west grows excellent stone fruit, but mild Perth winters mean low chill, so variety choice matters more here than almost anywhere else.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west coastal belt has hot dry summers and mild wet winters that suit stone fruit, but the mild winters on the Perth plain deliver only low winter chill. That favours low-chill Japanese plums and the Florida-bred Gulf series close to the coast, while the higher-chill European plums and prunes do better in the cooler Perth Hills and the far south-west. As a rule, stone fruit dominates the warmer districts and pome fruit the cooler southern hills.

sections.heading: Where plums grow in WA
sections.body: The Perth Hills around Pickering Brook, Karragullen, Roleystone, Bickley and Carmel are the closest orchard district to the city, growing plums alongside peaches and nectarines. Donnybrook, south of Perth, is a long-established stone-fruit and pome-fruit town, and the cooler far south-west around Manjimup, Pemberton and Balingup picks up the extra winter chill that suits European types. Many Perth backyards also carry heavy-bearing low-chill plums.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's summer heat brings an early harvest. Low-chill Japanese plums can start from about November or December on the warm coastal plain, the main-season Japanese plums run through December and January, and the cooler hills and the far south-west, along with the European types, carry the season into March and April.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Western Australia's signature fruit pest is the Mediterranean fruit fly (medfly), which is established right across the south-west and readily attacks ripening plums, so baiting and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit are essential. Queensland fruit fly, the pest of the eastern states, is not established in WA. The dry summers keep brown rot in check compared with the humid east, though it can still appear in a wet spring or in the wetter south. Rainbow lorikeets and other birds will strip a backyard plum, so net trees as the fruit colours.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually treated and certified before they cross the border, and stone-fruit (Prunus) nursery stock falls under those rules. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post plum trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Can plums cope with Perth's sandy soil?
faqs.a: Yes, with help. Dig plenty of compost into Perth's free-draining sand, mulch thickly to hold moisture over the shallow roots, and water deeply and often through summer, since a plum that dries out while fruiting drops its crop and sizes small.

faqs.q: If I have room for only one plum tree in Perth, which should I plant?
faqs.a: Santa Rosa. It is partially self-fertile, so it crops on its own, and it pollinates other Japanese plums as well. A self-fertile European plum, or a multi-graft tree carrying two compatible varieties, are the other one-spot options.

QLD.intro: Growing plums in Queensland. The state splits sharply by winter chill, so where you are decides which plums will fruit: the cool Granite Belt grows the full range, while the warm coast needs low-chill varieties.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The elevated Granite Belt around Stanthorpe gets real winter chill and frost and is Queensland's traditional deciduous stone-fruit country, so it grows standard Japanese plums and even some higher-chill types. Most of subtropical and coastal south-east Queensland is low in winter chill, so growers there must stick to low-chill Japanese plums, especially the Florida-bred Gulf series (Gulf Ruby, Gulf Gold, Gulf Blaze, Gulf Beauty). The humid wet tropics of the far north are generally too warm and wet for plums.

sections.heading: Where plums grow in QLD
sections.body: The Granite Belt and Southern Downs (Stanthorpe, Applethorpe and Ballandean), sitting around 800 metres and higher, is the main deciduous stone-fruit district and the only part of the state cold enough for the full range of plums. Across the subtropical coast and hinterland, from Brisbane to the Sunshine Coast, plums are grown only as low-chill backyard trees.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Low-chill plums on the warm coast and lowlands ripen very early, often from October through December, ahead of the rest of the country. The cooler Granite Belt picks a few weeks to a couple of months later, from about mid-November into January.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across the state and is the major pest of plums, stinging ripening fruit through mid and late summer, so trapping, baiting and netting are part of growing here. Brown rot is the key disease and is worse under Queensland's humidity and summer rain, especially close to harvest, so an open tree, orchard hygiene and the removal of rotted mummies all help.

faqs.q: Can I grow plums in Brisbane or on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Yes, if you choose a low-chill variety. The Florida-bred Gulf series (Gulf Ruby, Gulf Gold, Gulf Blaze, Gulf Beauty) need only around 250 to 400 hours of winter chill and fruit well in subtropical south-east Queensland, where high-chill plums would barely flower.

faqs.q: Do plums need a second tree to fruit in Queensland?
faqs.a: Usually. Apart from partially self-fertile Santa Rosa, Japanese plums need a compatible Japanese variety flowering at the same time, so in a low-chill Queensland garden plant two low-chill types or a multi-graft. Japanese and European plums do not pollinate one another.

NSW.intro: Growing plums in New South Wales. The state runs from cool, high tablelands to a warm, humid coast, so the right plum depends very much on your district and its winter chill.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The cool Central Tablelands around Orange and Bathurst and the Young district on the South West Slopes get plenty of winter chill and grow standard Japanese plums and the higher-chill European plums and prunes. The warm, irrigated Riverina grows stone fruit commercially, while the humid, low-chill Sydney Basin and coast suit low-chill Japanese plums and the Gulf series. Match the variety's chill rating to your district.

sections.heading: Where plums grow in NSW
sections.body: The Central Tablelands (Orange, Bathurst and Cowra) is the heart of the state's cool-climate orchard country, and the Young and Hilltops district on the South West Slopes grows plums alongside its famous cherries. The Riverina around Griffith grows stone fruit under irrigation, and the Sydney Basin and Hawkesbury carry low-chill backyard plums.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: New South Wales picks plums across summer, roughly December to March. The warm Riverina and the low-chill coast come in earliest, from about December, while the cool Central Tablelands around Orange and Bathurst ripen later, into February and March.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across New South Wales and is the main pest of plums, so trapping and baiting matter from late spring onwards. Brown rot is the key fungal disease, rotting blossom in spring and fruit near harvest, and is driven by warm, wet weather; the brown rot in Australian orchards is caused by Monilinia fructicola and M. laxa. Leaf rust can also spot the foliage of plums and prunes in warmer or inland districts.

faqs.q: How do I protect plum blossom from frost on the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: The cold tablelands give plums plenty of chill, but early blossom can be caught by spring frost. Plant on a rise where cold air drains away, lean toward a later-flowering variety, and be ready to cover a young tree on a frosty night.

faqs.q: Which plums are easiest on the warm, humid NSW coast?
faqs.a: Low-chill Japanese plums fruit best where winters are mild, but the coast's humidity drives brown rot, so plant for full sun and airflow, prune to an open vase, and clean up fallen and mummified fruit to break the disease cycle.

VIC.intro: Growing plums in Victoria. The cool temperate climate gives reliable winter chill, which makes Victoria one of the best states for the full range of plums, including the European prunes and gages.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cool winters easily supply the chill that plums need, so the state grows both the juicy Japanese plums and the higher-chill European plums and prunes that want around 800 hours or more. The irrigated Goulburn Valley is the stone-fruit heartland, the hot dry Sunraysia grows fruit under irrigation, and the cooler Yarra Valley and southern districts ripen later. Frost on early blossom is the main weather risk.

sections.heading: Where plums grow in VIC
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley around Shepparton, Cobram and Ardmona is Victoria's stone-fruit heartland and one of the largest stone-fruit districts in the country. Sunraysia and Swan Hill around Mildura grow stone fruit along the Murray under irrigation, while the cool Yarra Valley and central Victoria ripen plums later in the season.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria harvests later and over a longer span than the warmer states, roughly December to April, because the cooler season ripens fruit slowly. Japanese plums run through December to March, and the European plums and prunes finish the season late, from February into April.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is now established in northern Victoria, including the Goulburn Murray Valley and the Sunraysia and Swan Hill districts, so Victoria can no longer be treated as fruit-fly-free and growers there trap and bait as a matter of course. Brown rot is the key disease, infecting blossom in spring and fruit near harvest in wet weather. Spring frosts can damage early plum blossom, and European wasps and birds go after ripe fruit.

faqs.q: Is Victoria free of fruit fly?
faqs.a: No. Queensland fruit fly is now established in northern Victoria, including the Goulburn Murray Valley around Shepparton and the Sunraysia district around Mildura, so plum growers there need to trap and bait. Much of Victoria was historically managed as a low-risk area, but that is no longer the case.

faqs.q: Which plums suit Victoria's cold winters?
faqs.a: All of them, which is the state's advantage: reliable winter cold ripens the juicy Japanese plums and the higher-chill European plums and prunes that milder states cannot finish. Pair varieties that flower together for good fruit set, and shelter early blossom from spring frost.
